cataloging_subject.label: The Three Kings of Cologne
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous standard Middle English prose translation and abridgment of the Historia trium regum attributed to John of Hildesheim, including the related recensions represented by Cambridge University Library MS Ee.4.32 and British Library Royal MS 18 A X. The cataloged work is distinct from John's Latin source work, the physical manuscript witnesses, Horstmann's 1886 parallel-text edition, its 1988 Kraus reprint, U-M's 1999 electronic encoding, and the repository XML artifact. It is also distinct from the independent Lambeth prose translation and the Middle English verse version that share forms of the same modern title.

catalog_summary.title: The Three Kings of Cologne
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator and adapter; from John of Hildesheim's Historia trium regum
catalog_summary.date: Conventionally dated around 1400; securely after the 1364-1375 Latin source and before the dated 1442 English witness
catalog_summary.region: England; exact place of translation not securely established

notes: This is the established scholarly title for the standard prose translation, not a claim that all witnesses transmit an identical medieval title.

titles.value: Historia trium regum. Middle English (standard prose version)
notes: The qualifier distinguishes this translation from the independent Lambeth prose translation and the Middle English verse version.

titles.value: The Three Kings of Cologne: An Early English Translation of the 'Historia Trium Regum'
notes: This is the shorter form of Horstmann's edition title and the U-M item title, not necessarily an original title of the medieval translation.

titles.value: Historia et vita trium regum Indie, viz. Melchior, Balthazar et Jaspar

scope.description: Explicit rubric in Cambridge University Library MS Ee.4.32

notes: Recorded as a witness title, not promoted to the sole preferred title of the cross-witness work.

agents.name: John of Hildesheim
notes: Attributed author of the Latin Historia trium regum and therefore source author, not author of the Middle English wording.

agents.name: Carl Horstmann
notes: Editor and collator of the 1886 EETS parallel-text edition used by the U-M encoding through its 1988 Kraus reprint.
No evidence supports attributing the standard prose translation to John of Hildesheim or to a named English translator.

responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous Middle English abridger and adapter

scope.description: The standard translation's archetypal abridgment of the Latin source

assertion.notes: Schaer argues that the abridged form was primary and that the surviving unabridged form is a later expansion, while leaving aspects of the prototype abridgment unresolved.

scope.description: Authorship of the Latin source work translated and adapted in The Three Kings of Cologne

notes: The responsibility is source-work authorship and does not assign the anonymous Middle English translation to John.

scope.description: The immediate 1886 source edition and its 1988 reprint
Parallel English texts from Cambridge Ee.4.32 and Royal 18 A X, with a supplied opening and editorial interventions
Variant apparatus comparing additional manuscript witnesses and the early Wynkyn de Worde print

date_statements.display: Around 1400; securely after the Latin source (1364-1375) and before the dated 1442 English witness

assertion.notes: The broad normalized interval records the hard termini, not an assertion that translation occupied that whole period. Schaer accepts Horstmann's conventional date around 1400 but stresses that the linguistic basis is not decisive.

notes: Do not substitute the fifteenth-century witness dates or the 1886, 1988, and 1999 edition and encoding dates for the work's translation date.
No exact place of translation is asserted. Witness dialects and copying places do not establish the place where the archetypal translation was made.

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Biblical phrases, rubrics, citations, and source-text quotations embedded within the Middle English translation

notes: The XML's German, Dutch, French, Danish, Spanish, and Italian language declarations describe Horstmann's introduction and apparatus, not substantive whole-work languages of the cataloged Middle English translation.

form_statements.description: A prose translation and abridgment; Latin quotations and rubrics do not change the work's discovery form.

subject_statements.label: The Biblical Magi and the Adoration of Christ
Lives, deaths, relics, and translations of the Three Kings
Epiphany devotion and the cult of the Three Kings
Medieval representations of Eastern geography, peoples, religions, and customs
notes: The work's medieval ethnographic claims are cataloged as subject matter, not endorsed as historical descriptions.

subject_statements.label: Prester John and Eastern Christian communities

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English prose translation and abridgment of John of Hildesheim's Historia trium regum. It begins with prophecies and traditions surrounding the Magi, narrates their journey to Bethlehem and Adoration of Christ, follows their later conversion, preaching, deaths, and association with Saint Thomas, and traces the legendary transfer of their relics through Constantinople and Milan to Cologne. Long digressions describe Eastern lands, products, Christian communities, religious customs, Prester John, and the cult of the Three Kings.

3KCol.xml does not encode a simple single-witness transcription. It reorganizes Horstmann's parallel print edition into separate Cambridge and Royal divisions, retains his English variant apparatus, and omits the printed Latin text and marginal synopsis. The two main English divisions represent related recensions of one standard prose translation rather than two unrelated authored works.

extent.description: One prose translation represented by two edited witness texts. The XML contains 78 DIV2 nodes: 38 under the Cambridge division (35 tagged chapter and 3 tagged section) and 40 U-M-supplied sections under the Royal division. It also contains 7 DIV1 nodes (contents, introduction, two manuscript-text divisions, and three variant-apparatus divisions), 225 PB elements, and 46 MILESTONE elements. These are encoding metrics, not a stable intrinsic chapter count; U-M explicitly supplied the Royal headings and some section boundaries.

content_parts.label: The Three Kings of Cologne
content_parts.summary: The complete standard Middle English prose translation, represented in the source chain by a Cambridge text and a separately revised Royal recension.

locators.value: begins fol. 87r; inferred through fol. 119r because the next item begins fol. 119v
parallel English texts on pp. 2-158; variants on pp. 159-205

notes: The Cambridge and Royal recensions are locators and textual versions of the whole, not intrinsic content parts to which one witness's date or dialect should be generalized.

related_works.title: Historia trium regum
notes: The Latin work attributed to John of Hildesheim was composed between 1364 and 1375. This relation does not claim that the English translator used any one surviving Latin manuscript.

related_works.title: The Three Kings of Cologne (independent Lambeth prose translation)
notes: The translation preserved chiefly in Lambeth Palace MS 491 and in an excerpt in Huntington HM 114 is an independent Middle English prose work, not another witness of 3KCol.

related_works.title: The Three Kings of Cologne (Middle English verse version)
notes: The fragmentary rhyme-royal version is a distinct Middle English translation and adaptation of the Latin work.

evidence.summary: Identifies U-M's 1999 encoding, the 1988 Kraus reprint of Horstmann's 1886 EETS edition, the Cambridge and Royal source texts, the variant apparatus, embedded provider terms, and the encoding's omissions and structural interventions.

evidence.citation: Element and attribute inspection of the checked-in 3KCol XML, performed 2026-07-11.
evidence.summary: Inspection found 7 DIV1, 78 DIV2, 225 PB, 46 MILESTONE, 410 P, and 1 NOTE elements. The Cambridge text has 38 DIV2 nodes (35 chapter, 3 section), 45 manuscript milestones, and 76 Latin FOREIGN spans; the Royal text has 40 section nodes, one initial folio milestone, and 58 Latin FOREIGN spans.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item 3KCol.
evidence.summary: Identifies the 1988 Kraus publication, Horstmann as editor, public full-text access, current public-domain and No Copyright-United States statements, and the encoded contents through the English variant apparatus.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., The Three Kings of Cologne: An Early English Translation of the 'Historia Trium Regum' by John of Hildesheim, EETS OS 85 (London: N. Trubner, 1886), Internet Archive item threekingscolog00horsgoog.
evidence.locator: Title page; introduction pp. v-xxi; parallel English texts pp. 2-158; variant apparatus pp. 159-205; Latin text pp. 206-312
evidence.summary: The inspected scan confirms title-page responsibility and publication, Horstmann's account of the translation and witnesses, the Cambridge and Royal parallel-text layout, the separately printed English variants and Wynkyn de Worde readings, and the Latin text omitted from the U-M encoding.

evidence.citation: Frank Schaer, The Three Kings of Cologne: A Diplomatic Edition of the Unabridged English Version of John of Hildesheim's Historia trium Regum in Durham MS Hunter 15 ... and a Study of the Manuscript Tradition, PhD thesis, University of Adelaide, submitted 1992 (degree 1993).
evidence.summary: Distinguishes the standard prose translation from the second prose and verse versions, supports the conventional c. 1400 date and hard termini, reports more than twenty witnesses, analyzes the Cambridge and Royal recensions, argues that the abridged form is primary, and identifies Horstmann's text as an approximate reconstruction rather than a simple diplomatic transcript.

evidence.citation: Margaret Connolly, 'Ee.4.32,' Index of Middle English Prose, Handlist XIX: Manuscripts in the University Library, Cambridge (Boydell & Brewer, 2009), pp. 117-118.
evidence.summary: Identifies Ee.4.32 part 1 as the standard Middle English prose translation, gives its incipit, explicit and folio extent, supplies the Latin explicit title, and confirms that Horstmann edited from this witness.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Royal MS 18 A X.
evidence.locator: Item 15, fol. 87; date and physical description
evidence.summary: Dates the vellum codex to the first half of the fifteenth century, locates the abridged Three Kings at fol. 87, identifies Horstmann's edition, describes the modified chapter openings that spell the Margareta Moningtown and Mawde Stranlea acrostic, and records the codex's 134-folio extent and physical request route.

open_questions.question: Can the date and place of the archetypal standard prose translation be narrowed beyond the conventional date around 1400 and a broad English origin?
next_steps: Compare current LALME and manuscript-localization work across the earliest independent witnesses rather than transferring one copy's dialect to the translation.
Review post-Schaer scholarship for new codicological or source-text termini.

open_questions.question: What precise form of the standard prose translation is closest to the archetype across the second-, third-, and Royal-recension branches?
next_steps: Use Schaer's stemma and apparatus to compare readings at the level of individual passages.
Do not equate Horstmann's Cambridge text, his Royal text, or the Durham unabridged witness with the archetype without qualification.

notes: The manifest describes the standard prose translation across its evidenced recensions while binding only the witnesses and edition layers directly relevant to the 3KCol source chain.
Witness dates, foliation, dialect, and image terms remain scoped to lineage entities and are not generalized to the abstract work.
